senses.definition: to respond to a person in authority, such as a parent or teacher, in a manner that is impolite or disrespectful

examples.x: The student was reprimanded for answering back to the teacher when asked to do their homework.
It's important for children to learn not to answer back to their parents when given instructions.
The employee was warned about answering back to their supervisor during a meeting.
She was known for her habit of answering back to her boss, which caused workplace tension.
His tendency to answer back to his coach led to his suspension from the team.

senses.definition: to make something or someone return or be returned to a particular place or condition

examples.x: The successful campaign brought back customers.
He decided to bring back the vintage car to its former glory.
The successful negotiations brought back peace to the region.
Efforts to conserve the natural habitat will help bring back the biodiversity of the area.
He brought back the book he borrowed last week.

senses.definition: to remember something or someone from the past
to return to a previous state or condition, often after a period of decline or loss

examples.x: After a tough season, the team managed to come back stronger than ever.
The company is trying to come back after its financial difficulties.
The patient made a remarkable recovery and came back to full health.
He came back to the project after taking some time off to focus on other things.

senses.definition: to reverse one's direction and return along the same route, often to retrace one's steps or evade pursuers

examples.x: Realizing they had taken the wrong path, the hikers had to double back to find the correct trail.
The suspect attempted to escape by doubling back through the alleys, trying to lose the pursuing officers.
After reaching a dead end, the group had no choice but to double back and find an alternate route.
To retrieve the misplaced keys, she had to double back to the coffee shop where she last remembered having them.
The runners decided to double back during the race to help a teammate who had fallen behind.

senses.definition: to respond to an email message by sending a reply or answer to the sender's original email address
(of movies, novels, etc.) to present a scene or sequence that depicts events in the past

examples.x: The TV series frequently flashed back to the characters' early relationships to reveal how they had evolved.
The novel effectively flashes back to the protagonist's youth, highlighting formative experiences.
The movie constantly flashes back to the character's early relationships, demonstrating how they evolved over time.
In the play, the scenes flash back to the characters' shared history, helping the audience understand their complex relationships.
The film cleverly flashed back to the character's childhood to reveal the origins of his phobias.

senses.definition: to have strong memories or emotions from the past come back suddenly and vividly

examples.x: When she visited her childhood home, memories of her youth flooded back.
The old photograph album made all the family stories flood back to him.
Hearing that song on the radio caused feelings of nostalgia to flood back.
As they walked through their old school, the memories of their school days flooded back.

senses.definition: to return to a place, state, or condition
to contact someone again later to provide a response or reply, often after taking time to consider or research the matter

examples.x: I'll get back to you with the information you requested as soon as I find it.
She promised to get back to her friend about their weekend plans.
I'll get back to you with the results of the analysis as soon as it's completed.
She's in the middle of a task but will get back to you as soon as she's finished.
The manager promised to get back to the employee with feedback on the project.

senses.definition: to return something that was previously received from a specific person or thing, such as money

examples.x: Can you please give back the pen I lent you yesterday?
She promised to give back the money she borrowed from her friend.
It's important to give back items in the same condition you received them.

senses.definition: to return a call or call someone again because one was not available the first time they called

examples.x: I rang back, but there was no answer on the other end.
If I don't answer, just leave a voicemail, and I'll ring back shortly.
Please leave a message, and I'll ring back when I'm done with this task.

senses.definition: to return something to its original location, owner, or starting point

examples.x: I'll take back the library books after finishing reading them.
She is taking back the rental car to the airport tomorrow.
They took back the canoe to the lake after their trip.
We were taking back the borrowed tools when it started raining.
He will take back the package to the post office if it's not delivered on time.

senses.definition: to respond to someone by sending a text message

examples.x: He always makes an effort to text back quickly.
Could you text back and let me know if you're available?

senses.definition: to call someone back in response to a missed call

examples.x: Can you try back the office later?
After the missed call, she decided to try back immediately.
The voicemail instructed me to leave my details for them to try back.

senses.definition: to return to a previous state or condition

examples.x: Despite her efforts to move forward, she couldn't shake off her doubts and found herself turning back to old habits.
The peace talks seemed promising at first, but recent events have caused negotiations to turn back to hostility.
The medication helped him improve temporarily, but soon he began to turn back to his previous condition.
